observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Accounting Office to take up the proposed adjusting journal entry:

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the General Services Office (GSO) Designate to prepare the IIRUP of  all the unserviceable PPEs with an initial aggregate cost of ₱1,292,154.00 in accordance with Section 79 of PD 1445 and Section 59 Chapter 2 of The New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual for LGUs Volume II, as this would serve as the basis of the Disposal Committee to dispose such and for the Accounting Office to drop the same in the books in order to correct the overstatement to PPE account and Government Equity account.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to record the depreciation expense of the Other Infrastructure Assets account and its corresponding accumulated depreciation in order to present fairly the value Other Infrastructure Assets account and to adhere with paragraphs 42 and 43 of IPSAS 17 and Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1.

We recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan consider the passage of a resolution recommending derecognition of the PPE currently used by the DepEd for approval of the Municipal Mayor; and
We also recommended that after approval, the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to take up in the books of accounts the following proposed adjusting journal entry to fairly present the balances of PPE and Government Equity accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the GSO Designate and the Inventory Committee to finish the physical count of inventory and reconcile with the records of the Accounting Office thereafter and effect the necessary adjustments, if any.

We therefore recommended that Local Chief Executive:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare a Journal Entry Voucher to adjust the under(over)statements made in recording various asset accounts, implementing the foregoing proposed adjusting journal entry:
Henceforth, strictly adhere with Section 4.1 of COA Circular 2015-009 ultimately complying with Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
require the Municipal Accountant to validate the claimants of the unpaid obligations in the amount of Ᵽ217,940.00 which remained outstanding for more than two years and once claimants and existence of liability could no longer be verified, a report thereon shall be provided to the Municipal Mayor that will be presented to the Sangguniang Bayan which will in turn be the basis of the latter to pass a resolution recommending the dropping of these obligations from the books to fairly present the balance of this account in the financial statements; and
instruct the Municipal Accountant to only recognize accounts payable based on valid claims and are properly substantiated with sufficient evidence.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Require the Municipal Accountant to submit immediately to the DOH-CHD-1 the required documents for the final release of the fund;
Confide with DOH CHD-I regarding the availability of new funds since the fund for the Construction of Sigay Super Health Center, originated from FY 2023 already expired per Section 3.3 of NBC 592 or the Guidelines on the release of Funds for FY 2024; and
Direct the focal persons for programs and projects funded by other agencies, to properly monitor the projects assigned to them to avoid encountering similar complications as this.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to return the aforementioned funds totaling ₱3,274,210.01 to the BTr, in order to comply with Section 1 of RA. No. 11519 dated December 29, 2020 “An Act Extending the Availability or Appropriation Under Republic Act (RA) No. 11494, Otherwise Known as The Bayanihan to Recover as One Act,” Section 3.5 of National Budget Circular No. (NBC) 587 dated January 3, 2022 and Supreme Court ruling under GR No. 208566, so that the funds can be appropriated by the government to other PPAs.

We reiterated our previous audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to return the unused balances of fund transfers to the SAs in the aggregate amount of ₱73,292.83 in compliance with Sections 4.9 and 6.7 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994, for the latter to utilize the same for other PPAs of other government agencies needing financial assistance.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to strictly observe chronological posting of accounts in the GL to enable easy generation of account balances anytime during the year for the preparation of reliable monthly trial balances and interim financial statements which are crucial in decision making.

We recommended that henceforth, the Municipal Mayor:
require the Municipal Treasurer and all officials and employees to observe strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and liquidate fully all cash advances at the end of the year or as soon as the purpose for which it was granted has been served, and
instruct the Municipal Accountant not to process claims for additional cash to any official or employee unless the previous cash advance given to him is first settled or a proper accounting thereof is made to avoid misappropriation and possible loss of government funds.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer to deposit collections intact with the authorized depository bank whenever she goes to the bank for the encashment of check/s to avoid the accumulation of huge cash in her possession thereby minimizing the risk of possible loss, misuse or misappropriation of government funds and at the same time enable the municipality to earn additional income in the form of interest on bank deposits.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Assessor to expedite the processing of TCT or OCT of the lot previously purchased by the Municipality in order to concur with the provision of Section 39.2 of P.D. 1445 so that absolute ownership can be secured over said property.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require all concerned Department Heads to facilitate the prompt implementation of all projects/programs funded out of the tobacco excise taxes in order to give the tobacco farmers who worked hard for the generation of the funds the early enjoyment of benefits they rightfully deserve.

We recommended that henceforth, the local Chief Executive require the Municipal Planning Development Coordinator and the implementing department to closely monitor the implementation of proposed priority programs/projects/activities under the 20% DF in order to deliver on time the benefits the constituents could derive therefrom and achieve socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer intensify the collection of real property taxes and conduct massive tax campaign in all the barangays. Furthermore, seek the cooperation of barangay officials since they have also shares on the collection of real property taxes. Thus, additional income would be collected to fund other priority programs and projects of the municipality which are beneficial to its constituent.

We reiterate our prior year’s audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the GAD Focal Point Officer to submit the GAD Plan and Budget to the COA Audit Team within five days from receipt of the DILG-endorsed GPB, and the GAD Accomplishment Report duly received by the DILG within five days from the end of January of the ensuing year so that deficiencies, if any, could be communicated to Municipality on time for appropriate corrective action.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that Local Chief Executive require the Chairperson of the GAD Focal Point System to fully implement programs/projects funded under the GAD Plan in order to fully address gender issues/problems of the constituents in the municipal level and to help the national government attain its goal of addressing gender issues in the country.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
1.11.1	Municipal Accountant to locate or trace the documents of recorded infrastructure assets and record the corresponding accumulated depreciation thereof; and
1.11.2 the Municipal Engineer to prepare the necessary documents outlining the information relative to the removed structures of road networks and furnish the same to the Municipal Accountant which will serve as her basis in determining the appropriate carrying amount of replaced parts of local road networks and other infrastructure assets to be derecognized from the books in order to fairly and reliably present these accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended and Management agreed that henceforth, the Municipal Mayor require the:
2.6.1	Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Accounting Office a schedule of unreleased checks at the end of every month or quarter, and
2.6.2	Municipal Accountant to restore the cash equivalent of all unreleased checks by debiting Cash in Bank – LCCA and crediting the appropriate payable/liability accounts supported with a working paper at the end of the month or quarter and by preparing a JEV at the end of the year based on the submitted schedule from the Municipal Treasurer.  In addition, in the ensuing year, prepare a JEV to reverse the entries made covering the restoration in the previous year.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to record in the books of accounts the fair market value of the two parcels of land in order to reflect the correct balances of the affected accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
5.4.1	instruct the Municipal Accountant to consider recording in the books of accounts the proposed adjusting journal entry by debiting Other Payables in the amount of ₱298,717.88 and crediting Prior Period Adjustment by the same amount for the incentives and cash prizes received by the municipality in the previous years, and
5.4.2	require the Municipal Accountant that henceforth be guided by the IPSAS Chart of Accounts in recording financial transactions in order to reflect the correct balances of accounts in the financial statements.

To rectify the aforementioned deficiencies, we recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
6.6.1	instruct the Municipal Accountant to see to it that all JEVs are duly supported with complete documents including explanations/computations to warrant easy verification on the validity and propriety of the transactions including the correctness of  accounting entries made, and
6.6.2	require the Municipal Accountant to follow the prescribed procedures in the recording of financial transactions from the JEVs to the corresponding journals then to the General Ledgers as embodied in Annexes 1 to 5 of Volume II of the MNGAS to ensure systematic verification of accounts presented in the financial statements.

We recommended and Management agreed that:
7.5.1 	the Sangguniang Bayan appropriate funds for the titling of the four parcels of land acquired many years ago, and
7.5.2	 the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Assessor to obtain TCT/OCT of all the parcels of land acquired by the municipality.

We recommended and Management agreed to stop the practice of awarding contracts to business entities whose members and officers  are municipal officials or employees  in order to promote transparency in any government contracts and eliminate any conflict of interest that might arise in giving considerations to favored suppliers and to avoid imposition of administrative, civil and criminal sanctions for Public Officials and Employees who will commit any violation from the prohibited acts provided in RA No. 6713, RA No. 7160 and PD No. 1445.

We recommended and Management agreed to inform the Auditor in writing on the posting of infrastructure project  billboards within ten days after the award of the contract and comply with the submission of monthly reports of on-going and completed contracts as required under COA Circular 2013-004 dated January 20, 2013, to promote transparency in the implementation of infrastructure projects and to ensure that the right of the people to information on matters of public concern is addressed.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to return the unused balances of fund transfers to the SA in the aggregate amount of ₱2,099,837.62 in compliance with Section 6.7 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994, for the latter to utilize the same for other PPAs of other government agencies needing financial assistance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
13.9.1	Municipal Human Resource Management Officer Designate to coordinate with the GSIS with regard to the unremitted premium contributions of the newly hired employees, salary differentials of promoted employees and salary increases, and
13.9.2	Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer to cause the immediate remittance of all GSIS premium contributions in compliance with the relevant provisions of RA No. 8291  in order not to deprive the concerned municipal officials and employees of their GSIS privileges and benefits and avoid the imposition of penalties.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
14.8.1	instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare immediately the disbursement vouchers for the remittance of the trust collections due to the Barangays in the total amount of   ₱192,929.66;
14.8.2	require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer that henceforth, release the share of each barangay on realty taxes and CTCs on a quarterly basis within five days after the end of each quarter to enable the latter to utilize the funds for its operations, and
14.8.3	require the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to make fund transfer of the RPT and CTC shares of the barangays including the municipal aid to every barangay from the bank account of the municipality to their corresponding bank account for safer, faster and easier remittance of shares and to avoid issuance of checks.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
16.4.1 instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare immediately the disbursement vouchers for the tax refunds and the Municipal Treasurer to prepare the  corresponding checks to enable the  payees to utilize the amounts due them; and
16.4.2  require the Municipal Accountant that henceforth,  tax refunds be given immediately to the concerned officials/employees in order not to deprive them of the use of the amount due them and to avoid frequent inclusion of the same in the schedule of Other Payables.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor:
17.5.1 require the Municipal Accountant to submit the original copy of the 78 cancelled checks for inspection and proper monitoring; and
17.5.2 instruct the Municipal Treasurer that henceforth, attach all the original copy of cancelled checks to the Report of Checks Issued and submit the same together with the cancelled checks to the Municipal Accountant who shall submit these documents to the Office of the Auditor.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
18.8.1	require the one in-charge in the issuance of Driver’s Trip Tickets to put serial number in chronological order on all Driver’s Trip Tickets and see to it that the same are approved by the authorized official before these are issued to the drivers;
18.8.2	instruct all drivers   not to drive municipal vehicles/trucks/ heavy equipment without duly approved Driver’s Trip Ticket and    fill out the corresponding data required therein before and after the trip, and
18.8.3	require the GSO and the Municipal Accountant to assist the drivers in the preparation of the Monthly Report of Official Travels    and instruct them to prepare the Report of Fuel Consumption for all vehicles/heavy-equipment owned by the municipality and submit the same to the Auditor within the first ten days of the succeeding month.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant that henceforth ensure that all revenue and disbursement documents including the corresponding journals shall be submitted to the Commission on Audit not later than the 10th day of the following month in order to permit timely post audit of these transactions so that deficiencies, if any, could be communicated to management on time for appropriate corrective action.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary documents for the application of his bond and the   GSO and pay the corresponding bond premiums in order to protect the interest of the government

We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the Monthly Report on the Sources and Utilization of DRRMF, furnish the same to the LDRRMO for the latter to reconcile with his record and submit to the COA Auditor not later than the 15th day after the end of each month so that errors/deficiencies could be communicated to Management on time for appropriate corrective action.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor require:
22.5.1	the Municipal Treasurer to transfer the cash allocated for Trust Liability – DRRMF from the bank account of General Fund to the bank account of Trust Fund in order to facilitate accounting and monitoring  of  said fund and avoid possible confusion on the usage of check booklet to be used, and
22.5.2	the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary accounting entries after the bank transfer in order to reflect the correct balances of Cash in Bank for each fund  and minimize  if not to eliminate  the balances of the Due to Other Funds and Due from Other Funds accounts.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Budget Officer to fill out all the data indicated in the CAFOA in order to ascertain that the expenditures being obligated are within the budget and are charged to the proper office, function/program/project and account code to avoid the incurrence of overdraft in appropriation and facilitate verification of the Statement of Comparison of Budget versus Actual Amounts.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
24.12.1	instruct the BAC to fast track the procurement process of all the programs/projects funded out of the RA No. 7171 funds which were not yet subjected to public bidding, and
24.12.2	require all concerned Department Heads to facilitate the prompt implementation of all projects/programs funded out of the tobacco excise taxes in order to give the tobacco farmers who worked hard for the generation of the funds the early enjoyment of benefits they rightfully deserve.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the head of office concerned to intensify the implementation of the priority programs/projects funded under the 20% Development Fund in order to fully contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes of the LGU.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
26.6.1	cause for the immediate refund of ₱350,454.69 spent for the 22 staff because the purpose of the travel could still be achieved by the 7 officials and 10 department heads, thus wastage of funds could have been avoided and such expenditure can be dispensed with without loss or damage to property, and
26.6.2	stop the practice of conducting Knowledge Management Journey or similar activity in the Visayas or Mindanao considering the scarce financial resources of the municipality.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor cause for the immediate utilization of funds for disaster victims in order to maximize its purpose of helping to alleviate the sufferings of the latter.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor:
28.3.1	require the Municipal Accountant,  the Municipal Treasurer  and the Head of Office in charge in the implementation of the project, program or activity to  make periodic report on the utilization of funds received for specific purpose as a guide of management in decision making, and

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the LCE to require the BAC and the latter committed to ensure that the bidding documents are duly accomplished showing all the important provisions and specific parameters required in a particular procurement activity in order to convey accurate information to prospective bidders, hence interested bidders may not be misled and avoid the risk that they may not meet the requirements of the contract or may not complete the contract in the expected completion date.

The recommended earliest possible time in the conduct of post-qualification activities was not complied with, hence casting doubt on the proper conduct of the same, thus the risk that the winning bidder may not qualify with all the requirements and conditions of the contract.

We recommended the LCE to require the BAC and the latter assured to conduct proper and meticulous post-qualification evaluation to ensure that all statements made and documents submitted by the bidder with the Lowest Calculated Bid/Highest Rated Bid are duly verified and validated in all respects based on the abovementioned non-discretionary criteria.

The awarding of the contract of the five procurement activities totaling ₱7,491,839.53 exceeded the 15 calendar day required period as prescribed in Section 37.1.2 and 37.1.3 of the 2016 Revised IRR of the RA 9184, resulting in the delay of the execution of the contract.

We recommended that the LCE require the BAC and the latter assured to issue the NOA within 15 calendar days from the determination by the BAC of the bidder with the LCRB in conformity with Section 37.1.2 and 37.1.3 of the 2016 Revised IRR of the RA 9184 to avoid the incurrence of delay in the execution of the contract and submit justification on the noted departure from the abovementioned provisions of RA 9184.

The perfection of the contract of the eight procurement activities aggregating ₱29,175,250.83 were beyond ten calendar days as prescribed in Section 37.2.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of the RA 9184, hence the incurrence of delay in the procurement process and in the implementation of the contract.

We recommended that the LCE require the BAC and the latter committed that contracts are signed within ten (10) calendar days from receipt by the winning bidder of the NOA in adherence to Section 37.2.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of the RA 9184 in order to avoid the incurrence of delay in the procurement process and in the implementation of the contract and submit justification on the noted deficiency.

The issuance of the Notice to Proceed (NTP) of the two contracts aggregating ₱10,134,139.47 was beyond seven (7) calendar days from the date of approval of the contract contrary to Section 37.4.1 of the RA 9184, hence causing delay in the implementation of the contract.

We recommended that the LCE require the BAC and the latter ensured that the issuance of NTP is within seven calendar days from the date of approval of the contract pursuant to Section 37.4.1 of the RA 9184 in order to avoid the incurrence of delay in the implementation of the contract and submit justification on the noted deviation.

The Performance Security posted for the procurement of 19 procurements for infrastructure and goods with a total contract cost of   ₱53,329,726.10 were not properly dated casting doubt on the validity and compliance with the pertinent provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of 9184.

We recommended that the LCE require the BAC and the latter assured to verify that the Performance Security are duly/properly dated and posted on the required period in compliance with Sections 37.2.1, 37.2.3 and 39.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of 9184 to guarantee the faithful performance by the winning bidder of its obligations.

The Net Financial Contracting Capacity (NFCC) of the 16 contracts for infrastructure projects aggregating ₱75,956,631.20 were not accurately computed thus, the basis for verifying the liquidity and capacity of the bidder to absorb additional obligations is affected.

We recommended that the LCE require the BAC and the latter assured to carefully verify/check the computation of submitted NFCC to ensure that the bidder has the capacity to finance and implement the bid contract.

The NOA of 16 procurements for infrastructures and goods with contract cost aggregating ₱50,241,817.29 were not posted in the PhilGEPS website within the reglementary period prescribed in Sections 37.1.6 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184, thus the objectives of the procurement law to promote transparency and efficiency in the conduct of procurement in the government was not served. 

We recommended that the LCE require the BAC Secretariat and the latter committed to post the NOA in the PhilGEPs on the prescribed period as required in Sections 37.1.6 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184, in order to conform with the objectives of the procurement law in promoting transparency and efficiency in the conduct of procurement activities.

The Statement of Single Largest Completed Contract (SLCC) of the six contracts aggregating ₱24,986,243.28 were not supported with a Certificate of Final Acceptance, thus casting doubt on the eligibility or competence of the contractors to perform the requirements of the contract.  Likewise, the project stated in the SLCC of the contract for the supply and delivery of materials for construction/repair of curing barn costing ₱2,995,640.00 did not qualify with the required 25% of the Approved Budget of Contract (ABC).

We recommended that the LCE require the BAC and the latter assured to require the contractors to support the SLCC with a Certificate of Final Acceptance, to determine the competence of the contractor to carry out the project.

Three procurement activities totaling ₱3,142,183.89 were not included in the submitted approved Annual Procurement Plan (APP) of the Municipality for CY 2022 contrary to Section 7 of the RIRR of RA 9184, hence casting doubt on the validity and legality of these transactions.

We recommended that the LCE require the BAC and the latter agreed to immediately submit the Supplemental Procurement Plan on the abovementioned disbursements to ascertain the validity and legality of these transactions, henceforth, ensure that a supplemental Project Procurement Management Plan (PPMP) is included in the amended APP duly approved by the Head of the Procuring entity (HOPE).

Management entered into contract despite the inability of the contractors to submit the certification of approval of the Construction Safety and Health Program (CSHP) from the Department of Labor and Employment DOLE on the three infrastructure projects costing ₱10,532,912.78 contrary to Sections 37.2.2 and 7.2.3 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184, hence the protection and welfare of workers employed and the general public within and around the construction worksite were not ensured.

We recommended that the LCE require the BAC and the latter assured strict compliance with the provisions set forth in Sections 37.2.2 and 37.2.3 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184 by requiring the submission of CSHP duly approved by the DOLE prior to the approval of the contract, to ensure the safety and protection of the workers employed as well as the general public. 

Inaccurate dates were indicated in the various reports and certifications of the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) on the stages of bidding processes, hence affecting the validity and truthfulness on the conduct of the same.

We recommended that the LCE require the BAC and the latter committed to submit the corrected and duly dated/filled out reports/documents of the abovementioned procurement activities and to ensure that all reports and relevant documents pertaining to the conduct of bidding proceedings are duly filled out and properly dated on the succeeding procurement activities, to ascertain the validity and propriety of the transactions.

Other noted deficiencies are as follows:

There was minor error in the computation of Bills of Quantities (BOQ) on the 17 contracts aggregating ₱112,554,193.61, 

We recommended that the LCE require the BAC and the latter assured the Audit Team to carefully verify the computation of the BOQ and require the preparation of detailed cost estimates in support to the ABC on the succeeding transactions.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive designate a new committee to conduct physical inventory of all the component of the Property, Plant and Equipment so as to vouch the correct valuation and serviceability of the Property, Plant and Equipment. Likewise, the Inventory Report should be submitted to the auditor concerned for verification not later than January 31 of each year.

We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer strictly comply with the provision stated above. Collections for the day should be deposited intact with the authorized depository bank. The balance of collections not deposited during the day due to cut-off should be deposited in the next working day to avoid risks from using the collection for paying obligations and expose the funds to risk from loss or theft.

We have recommended that management immediately increase the fidelity bond of the Cashier or upon renewal of the same and the maximum cash accountability be adjusted accordingly pursuant to Treasury Circular No. 02-2009.

We have recommended that management remit immediately the compulsory premium contributions and all loan payments to the GSIS within the first ten (10) days of the calendar month following the month to which the contributions apply. Management should ensure strict observance of the provisions of the R.A 8291 to avoid interest and penalties for the unremitted contributions. Any differences noted from the agency remittances with the records of the GSIS should be reconciled or settled immediately.

We have recommended that the municipal government consider the benefits they could derive from insuring their properties with the “Property Insurance Fund” under the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS), to indemnify the government of any damage to, or loss of, its properties due to fire, earthquake, storm or other calamities.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor see to it that all employees will accomplish their daily time record to show that their services had been actually rendered in their claim for salaries. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer shall require duly accomplished/approved daily time records together with travel order/s, approved application for leave and medical certificates if needed before payment of salaries and wages is made to avoid doubt on the validity of the claim.

We have recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) evaluate strictly the technical documents submitted by the prospective bidders as provided in Section 23.5.1.3 of R.A. 9184 (2009) as amended in Section 23.4.1.3 of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. 9184 to avoid doubts in the eligibility of the bidder and must be supported with copies of contracts and other pertinent documents needed in the evaluation of technical documents. If after the eligibility check was conducted and all the bidders failed to meet the eligibility requirements, the BAC shall declare failure of bidding.

We have recommended that management strictly adhere to the regulation in the submission of all approved government contracts within the prescribed period of five (5) days as provided for in COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 in order to allow the Auditor to conduct timely review and evaluation of the submitted contracts. Also, the auditor can call the immediate attention of management regarding defects and deficiencies noted in the contract and suggest such corrective measures.

We have recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee evaluate the completeness of Bidding Documents, Eligibility and Technical Requirements and other contract documents (Sec 37.2.3 RA 9184) before recommending award of contracts to the Head of the Procuring Entity. Furthermore, the Municipal Accountant should require all pertinent documents before certifying the disbursement voucher as to the completeness of the supporting documents to avoid doubts on the validity and propriety of the transactions because of the deficiencies in the documentary requirements. Lacking documents must be submitted immediately to avoid issuance of Notice of Suspension.

We have recommended that management implement projects as planned and address the causes of the delay in the implementation of the development projects in order to maximize the utilization of the fund as intended and ultimately achieve desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes in the municipality as provided under DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011.

Likewise, the latest policies and guidelines on study tour activities of local government units as discussed in JM Circular No. 02 dated September 23, 2016 must be adhered to prevent incurrence of irregular, unnecessary excessive or extravagant expenditures or uses of government funds.

We have recommended that the GAD Focal Person of the municipality submit copy of the Accomplishment Report to the auditor to determine whether gender issues are effectively and efficiently addressed by GAD interventions in the agency.

We have recommended that management immediately enforce the settlement of the audit suspensions and disallowances as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009. Moreover, we recommend management to implement the Notice of Finality of Decision once issued.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that all copies of approved government contracts, purchase orders and their supporting documents be submitted to the auditor within five (5) working days upon approval for review to avoid doubts on the validity of the contracts and to ensure the submission of all the necessary supporting documents.
observations.managementAction: 2015 AAR is in the process of improving their internal control in procurement.
observations.reason: Verification of submitted documents if it include approved contracts for contract review.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Budget Officer control and monitor the issuance of Obligation Request (OBR) for every transaction. Obligation Request (OBR) should be made available prior to the issuance of Invitation to Bid and should be forwarded to the Head of the Procuring Entity before entering into a contract to prove that there is available appropriation and there is authority issued by the Local Chief Executive (LCE) to incur obligations. Likewise, only one Obligation Request (OBR) should be prepared for each contract.
observations.managementAction: 2015 AAR issued one OBR only for every contract with partial/progress billings.
observations.reason: Verification of issuance of OBRs for each contract with partial/progress billings.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended the Municipal Accountant to require all the necessary documents before certifying the Disbursements Voucher as to completeness of the supporting documents to avoid doubts on the legality, validity and propriety of the transactions. The Summary of Material Tests Performed should be submitted to help the municipality in the determination of the quality of materials being used in the project.
observations.managementAction: 2015 AAR requires the submission of the Summary of Material Test for the payment of infrastructure projects.
observations.reason: Verification of all payments for infrastructure projects if supported with summary of Material Test Performed.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended management to obtain all procurements of common-used supplies to the PS-DBM pursuant to Section 1 and 4 of the Administrative Order No. 17 dated July 28, 2011 to assure that the cost of procured items are reasonable and advantageous to management. If the required stocks are not available, management may resort to other mode of procurement pursuant to R.A. 9184 and its implementing rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: 2015 AAR purchased some of the common-used supplies through bidding / shopping if not available in PS-DBM.
observations.reason: Items procured were through bidding / shopping if not available in PS-DBM.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended the Municipal Accountant to strictly enforce the withholding of at least ten percent (10%) retention money of the total contract price from the payments made to suppliers or require a special bank guarantee before the release of the said amount after the lapse of the warranty period to assure that in case there will be manufacturing defects from the deliveries, the supplier will be held responsible for correcting the said defects.
observations.managementAction: 2015 AAR invites in writing three observers attached warranty security for procurement of goods in an amount equivalent to at least one percent (1%) of the total contract price.
observations.reason: Verification of procurement of goods if warranty security revealed that no retention money was deducted for the purchase of non-expandable supplies.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended management to ensure strict observance of the provisions of Section 54.1 of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. 9184 in the procurement of goods and services. Also, purchases should be in accordance with law, rules and regulations, with a view of ensuring efficiency, economy and effectiveness in its operations. Further, avoid non-consolidation of requisitions, issuance of two or more purchase orders and division of payments for single purchase order.
observations.managementAction: 2015 AAR consolidated their Vouchers requisitions for the procurement of goods.
observations.reason: Verification of Disbursement Vouchers proved that there were no requisitions for the splitting of procurement contracts/goods.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) should invite in writing three (3) observers 1.) A representative of the COA 2.) At least one (1) from a recognized private group and 3.) The other from a non-government organization (NGO). Observers shall be invited at least three (3) days before the date of the procurement stage/activity to enhance the transparency of the procurement process.

observations.reason: Verification of the Invitation to observers attached in some of the stages of the procurement process revealed that invitations in writing to three (3) observers were done.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Government strictly adhere on the proper implementation of the guidelines on designation as provided on the above cited CSC Memorandum Circular to avoid doubts on the validity of the designation order and the entitlement of the designee to the allowances attached to the position for Division Chief level positions and equivalent such as the Representation and Transportation Allowance (RATA).

observations.reason: Verification of various claims of the municipality if it includes RATA for designees.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended the Municipality to appropriate for the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (MDRRMF) amounting to not less than five percent (5%) of the estimated revenue from regular sources to fully achieve the objective of mainstreaming disaster risk reduction and lessening the socioeconomic and environmental impacts of disasters at all levels in the local community. The allocation for the LDRRMF shall cover the thirty percent (30%) lump-sum allocation for Quick Response Fund (QRF) and seventy percent (70%) allocation for disaster prevention and mitigation, preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery.
observations.reason: Verification of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF) revealed that for the 30% of the fund LDRRMF was allocated for Quick Response Fund (QRF).
